1: hāḇāh-lî ḇānîm wĕʾim-ʾayin mētāh ʾānōḵî — "Give me children, or I will die!" Rachel's outcry frames barrenness as mortal threat. Mētāh ʾānōḵî ("I am dying/I will die") uses the Qal participle — ongoing state of dying rather than a sudden death threat. In the ANE, barrenness was social death: a woman without children had no standing in the household, no security in old age, no memorial after death. Rachel's "I will die" is existential, not merely dramatic. Qānnēʾ — "she was jealous/envied" (Piel of qānāʾ, H7065) — the same verb used for the Philistines' envy of Isaac (26:14) and for God's jealousy (ʾēl qannāʾ, Exod 20:5). The word is strong: active, consuming rivalry.
2: hătaḥat ʾĕlōhîm ʾānōḵî ʾăšer mānaʿ mimmēḵ pĕrî-ḇāṭen — "Am I in the place of God, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?" Jacob's rhetorical question is theologically precise: taḥat ʾĕlōhîm (in the place of God) — Jacob cannot grant what God alone controls. Mānaʿ (H4513, to withhold, restrain, deny) — the same verb as in 1 Sam 1:5-6 for Hannah's barrenness (YHWH had closed her womb). Pĕrî-ḇāṭen — "fruit of the womb" — pĕrî (H6529, fruit) applied to the womb uses agricultural language for human fertility (cf. Ps 127:3: "children are a heritage of YHWH, a fruit of the womb his reward"). Jacob's angry response is theologically correct but pastorally tone-deaf — he names the theological truth without the compassion the situation requires.
3: wĕtēled ʿal-birkkāy wĕʾibbāneh gam-ʾānōḵî mimmennāh — "and she will bear on my knees, and I too will be built up through her." The phrase yālaḏ ʿal-birkkāy ("bear on my knees") — a ritual of adoption in the ANE; the one on whose knees the child is placed legally acknowledges the child as their own (cf. 50:23 where Joseph's grandchildren are placed on his knees). Rachel's proposal activates the legal surrogate-mother practice documented in Nuzi tablets. ʾIbbāneh — "I will be built up/have a house" — the Niphal of bānāh (to build), the same root as bēn (son) — a wordplay: "I will be built/have sons" (bānāh/bēn). The social "building" of a household through children is the structural logic of Levirate marriage and surrogate motherhood in the Torah.
4: wayyāḇōʾ ʾēleyhā yaʿăqōḇ — "Jacob slept with her." The narrative compression is characteristic: the event that will generate Dan is stated in five Hebrew words. The narrator records the legal and genealogical outcomes without lingering on the personal dynamics. Bilhah's voicelessness throughout this chapter — she is given, slept with, conceives, bears — contrasts with the vocal theological commentary of Leah and Rachel in naming their children. The matriarchal naming-speeches dominate; the surrogate mothers are recorded by function, not by speech.
5: wattar ḇilhāh waṯṯēled lĕyaʿăqōḇ bēn — "Bilhah conceived and bore Jacob a son." The formulaic birth-record (hārāh... wayyēled) — "conceived and bore" — is the standard genealogical formula of Genesis. Each of the twelve sons follows a variation of this formula, linking the patriarchal family to the creation-genealogy pattern of chs 5 and 10. The use of Jacob's name (lĕyaʿăqōḇ) rather than "her husband" specifies the genealogical fact: the child belongs to Jacob, legally through the surrogate arrangement.
6: dānannî ʾĕlōhîm wĕgam šāmaʿ bĕqōlî wayyittēn-lî bēn ʿal-kēn qārĕʾāh šĕmô dān — "God has vindicated me — he has also heard my voice and given me a son. Therefore she named him Dan." Dānannî — "God has judged/vindicated me" — the Qal perfect of dîn (H1777, to judge, to argue a legal case, to vindicate). Dan's name encodes a legal verdict: God has ruled in Rachel's favor in the rivalry with Leah. The term dîn is used for judicial advocacy throughout the OT (Ps 9:4; 72:4; 82:3). Rachel frames her childlessness as a legal dispute and God's response as judicial vindication. The combination — God judged AND heard — links to the Simeon-naming pattern (YHWH heard Leah's affliction) and shows the two wives appealing to the same divine judicial-compassionate register.
7: wattar ʿôḏ waṯṯēled bilhāh šipĕḥat rāḥēl bēn šēnî lĕyaʿăqōḇ — "Rachel's servant Bilhah conceived again and bore Jacob a second son." The formulaic repetition — "again... bore a second son" — follows the same narrative economy as the Leah-birth sequence. Bilhah's two births are compressed; the theological significance is in Rachel's naming speech in v8, not in the births themselves. The count (šēnî, second) establishes the number of children the surrogate arrangement has produced before Rachel changes strategy.
8: naptûlê ʾĕlōhîm niptaltî ʿim-ʾăḥōtî gam-yāḵoltî wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô naptālî — "With God's wrestlings I have wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed. So she named him Naphtali." The name naptālî from pātal (H6617, to twist, wrestle, be tortuous) — a rare verb suggesting both physical struggle and moral entanglement. The phrase naptûlê ʾĕlōhîm (wrestlings of God) uses the same root that will appear at Peniel (32:25,28 — though there the verb is ʾāḇaq, to wrestle). The "god-wrestlings" construction uses ʾĕlōhîm as an intensifier (a Hebraism for superlative degree: "tremendous/mighty wrestlings") — the same usage as "mountains of God" (Ps 36:7) for mighty mountains. Rachel's wrestling with her sister through proxy-births anticipates Jacob's wrestling with God — the same family pattern of striving expressed at the personal and cosmic levels.
9: waṯṯiqqaḥ ʾet-zilpāh šipĕḥātāh waṯṯittēn ʾōtāh lĕyaʿăqōḇ lĕʾiššāh — "Leah took her servant Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as a wife." Leah's response to her own cessation of bearing is to mirror Rachel's strategy — giving her servant Zilpah as a surrogate. The symmetry is structural: Rachel → Bilhah → Dan and Naphtali; Leah → Zilpah → Gad and Asher. Four sons are born to the two maidservants, paralleling the four sons already born to Leah directly. The twelve tribes emerge from this pattern of competition, surrogacy, and divine provision — a deeply human process that generates the covenant genealogy.
10: waṯṯēled zilpāh šipĕḥat lēʾāh lĕyaʿăqōḇ bēn — "Leah's servant Zilpah bore Jacob a son." The birth-formula is at its most compressed — no "conceived" (hārāh), just "bore." The formulaic economy accelerates as the birth-sequence multiplies. The twelve-tribe genealogy that will become Israel is generated in a narrative that is both economically told and humanly fraught — the tension between the personal pain of the mothers and the cosmic purpose of the genealogy is held together by the terse narrative style.
11: waṯṯōʾmer lēʾāh bāgāḏ wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô gāḏ — "Leah said, 'Good fortune!' and she named him Gad." The exclamation bāgāḏ (or bā gāḏ — "fortune has come") uses gāḏ (H1409, fortune, luck). The Qere/Ketiv textual tradition has two readings: the written text has bāgāḏ (as a single word) while the marginal note reads bā gāḏ (fortune has come). The name Gad is associated with the Canaanite god of fortune in the ancient Near East — though Leah's statement does not invoke the deity but uses the common noun. Isa 65:11 condemns those who "set a table for Gad" — the deity of fortune — suggesting the Canaanite background is present in the name even when not invoked theologically.
12: waṯṯēled zilpāh šipĕḥat lēʾāh bēn šēnî lĕyaʿăqōḇ — "Leah's servant Zilpah bore Jacob a second son." The second birth through Zilpah is noted with the same compressed formula as the first. The narrative's brevity for the surrogate births contrasts with the extended naming speeches for Leah's direct births — the maidservants' children are genealogically registered but narratively backgrounded. The number "second" (šēnî) signals completeness of the Zilpah sequence: two through each maidservant is the pattern.
13: waṯṯōʾmer lēʾāh bĕʾošrî kî ʾišĕrûnî ḇānôt wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô ʾāšēr — "Leah said, 'Happy am I! For women will call me happy.' So she named him Asher." ʾĀšēr from ʾeśer (H835, happiness, blessedness, the state of being fortunate) — used in the plural-of-excellence ʾašrê ("Blessed is the one who..."), the beatitude formula of the Psalms (Ps 1:1; 2:12; 32:1-2; etc.). The social dimension — "women will call me happy" (ʾišĕrûnî ḇānôt) — shows that Leah's sense of blessedness is validated by female community recognition; her social standing is being restored through sons. The Asher tribe will receive Moses's blessing: "Let Asher be blessed more than other sons... dip his foot in oil" (Deut 33:24).
14: wayyēlek rĕʾûḇēn bîmê qĕṣîr-ḥiṭṭîm wayyimṣāʾ dûḏāʾîm baśśāḏeh — "During the wheat harvest Reuben went and found mandrakes in the field." Dûḏāʾîm (H1736, mandrakes — Mandragora officinarum) — a plant with a forked root that resembles a human figure; the name may derive from dôḏ (H1730, beloved, uncle, love-token), connecting mandrakes to love and sexuality. Mandrakes were widely believed in the ANE and Mediterranean world to be aphrodisiacs and fertility stimulants — the Mishnah and Pliny both reference their use. The timing — wheat harvest — dates this to May/June. Reuben's discovery by his firstborn son is significant: the elder (who will lose his birthright) brings the fertility plant that will ironically not help Rachel (her children come from God, not mandrakes) but will help Leah conceive again.
15: halĕʾāṭ qāḥalt ʾet-ʾîšî wĕlāqaḥat gam ʾet-dûḏāʾê bĕnî — "Was it not enough that you took my husband? Will you also take my son's mandrakes?" Leah's accusation — "you took my husband" (qāḥalt ʾet-ʾîšî) — is the bitterest statement of the Rachel-Leah rivalry: Jacob's love for Rachel constitutes a form of husband-taking from Leah's perspective. The subsequent arrangement (Rachel trades Jacob's conjugal time for mandrakes) is darkly ironic: Rachel gets the reputed fertility plant but will only conceive through divine action (v22); Leah, who gives up the mandrakes, conceives immediately from the traded conjugal night (v17). The mandrakes are shown to be irrelevant — it is God who opens and closes wombs.
16: śāḵōr śĕḵartîḵā bĕdûḏāʾê bĕnî — "I have hired you with my son's mandrakes." The emphatic infinitive absolute śāḵōr śĕḵartîḵā (hiring I have hired you) — Leah addresses Jacob with the same assertiveness Rachel showed in v1 ("give me children"). The word śāḵōr/śāḵar (H7936/H7939, to hire, wages) is the commercial-contract term that dominates the Jacob-Laban narrative (29:15; 30:28,32-33; 31:7,41). Leah now applies it to her own marriage: she has "hired" her husband's conjugal attention with mandrakes. The irony of the commercial language applied to marriage is the narrator's sharp commentary on the state of the household.
17: wayyišmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm ʾel-lēʾāh waṯṯahar waṯṯēled lĕyaʿăqōḇ bēn ḥămîšî — "God heard Leah, and she conceived and bore Jacob a fifth son." The divine hearing (šāmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm) directly following the mandrake-for-conjugal-night exchange makes the theological point: God's hearing is what produces the conception, not the mandrakes Rachel has just obtained. The fifth son is the fifth direct birth Leah produces — she has now borne more children than any other woman in Jacob's household. God's consistent attention to the unloved wife is one of Genesis's sustained theological statements about divine preferential care for the vulnerable.
18: nātan ʾĕlōhîm śĕḵārî ʾăšer nātattî šipĕḥātî lĕʾîšî wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô yiśśāḵār — "God has given me my wages because I gave my servant to my husband. So she named him Issachar." Yiśśāḵār — the etymology here is folk: "there is reward/wages" (yēš śāḵār) or "man of wages" (ʾîš śāḵār). Leah interprets the birth as divine payment (śāḵar, H7939) for giving Zilpah as surrogate. The commercial vocabulary returns: the birth is śāḵār (wages), as if God has settled the labor account. The Issachar tribe receives a distinctive blessing in Gen 49:14-15 — a strong donkey crouching between two burdens, accepting conscript labor — which may echo the labor/wages etymology of the name.
19: waṯṯahar ʿôḏ lēʾāh waṯṯēled bēn-šiššî lĕyaʿăqōḇ — "Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son." The sixth son of Leah (including two through Zilpah) — the number "six" (šiššî) becomes part of Leah's naming speech in v20: "because I bore him six sons." The count matters: at six sons, Leah has generated more than half the tribal fathers of Israel through her own household (direct + surrogate). The sixth birth marks the completion of Leah's bearing sequence before the birth of Dinah (v21).
20: zĕḇālanî ʾĕlōhîm ʾōtî zeḇed ṭôḇ happaʿam yizĕḇĕlēnî ʾîšî kî-yālaḏtî lô šiššāh ḇānîm wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô zĕḇûlûn — "God has endowed me with a precious endowment. This time my husband will honor/dwell with me." Zĕḇûlûn — from zāḇal (H2082, to dwell, to honor/exalt); the name plays on zeḇed (H2065, a gift/endowment) and zāḇal. Two meanings operate: (1) God has gifted me (zeḇed ṭôḇ, a good gift); (2) my husband will now honor/dwell with me (yizĕḇĕlēnî). Leah's hope for Jacob's honor/habitation — articulated at son #1 (Reuben: "now my husband will love me") and again at son #6 — shows that the desire for covenantal conjugal presence remains unfulfilled. The six sons have not secured what Leah ultimately wants.
21: wĕʾaḥar yālaḏāh bat waṯṯiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmāh dînāh — "Afterwards she bore a daughter and named her Dinah." The one daughter named in the Jacob narrative — Dînāh, from dîn (to judge) — is introduced without birth-speech or etymology, in contrast to every son. The brevity of her introduction contrasts with the extended narrative she will receive in ch 34 (Dinah and Shechem). Her name's root (dîn, judgment) anticipates the violent "judgment" her brothers will enact on Shechem. The fact that she is named at all, while other daughters are not mentioned (31:41 says Jacob had daughters), marks her as significant for the subsequent narrative.
22: wayyizkor ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-rāḥēl wayyišmaʿ ʾēleyhā ʾĕlōhîm wayyipteaḥ ʾet-raḥmāh — "God remembered Rachel; God heard her and opened her womb." The formula wayyizkor ʾĕlōhîm (God remembered) — the zāḵar (H2142) covenant-memory verb — is used for God's response to covenant distress: 8:1 (God remembered Noah), 19:29 (God remembered Abraham for Lot), Exod 2:24 (God remembered the covenant with Abraham/Isaac/Jacob for enslaved Israel). Rachel's remembrance follows the same covenantal pattern: the memory is not mere recollection but active intervention. The triad — remembered / heard / opened — compresses three divine actions into one verse. The "opening" of the womb (peṯaḥ raḥmāh) uses the same verb as in 29:31 for Leah.
23: wathar waṯṯēled bēn waṯṯōʾmer ʾāsap ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ḥerpātî — "She conceived and bore a son and said, 'God has taken away my reproach.'" ʾĀsap ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ḥerpātî — "God has gathered/removed my reproach." ʾĀsap (H622, to gather, remove, take away) — one of the Joseph etymologies; the other is in v24 (yāsap = may he add). Ḥerpāh (H2781, reproach, disgrace) — in the ANE and OT, barrenness was experienced as social shame/reproach: the barren woman had failed her primary social duty. Hannah uses the same word (1 Sam 1:6-11); Elizabeth in Luke 1:25 ("he has taken away my reproach among men") uses the same theological language. The removal of reproach is divine vindication of the woman the social order had judged as deficient.
24: waṯṯiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô yôsēp lēʾmōr yōsēp YHWH lî bēn ʾaḥēr — "She named him Joseph, saying, 'May YHWH add to me another son.'" The name yôsēp encodes two etymologies simultaneously: (1) from ʾāsap (v23, "God has removed my reproach") — the ʾāsap root; (2) from yāsap (H3254, to add, to continue) — the prayer for a second son. The name is a bilingual pun: it looks backward (reproach removed) and forward (may God add more). The two-directional naming formula is unique in the Genesis birth-speeches. Joseph's name thus carries both gratitude and hope — which characterizes his entire narrative: what is lost is restored and multiplied. YHWH's name is invoked — the covenant name — marking the prayer as more than a fertility wish.
25: wayhî kaʾăšer yālaḏāh rāḥēl ʾet-yôsēp wayyōʾmer yaʿăqōḇ ʾel-lāḇān šallĕḥēnî wĕʾēlĕḵāh ʾel-mĕqômî ûlĕʾarṣî — "When Rachel had borne Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, 'Send me away that I may go to my place and to my land.'" The birth of Joseph is the trigger for Jacob's departure request — suggesting that in Jacob's mind, Joseph's birth completed a cycle. The phrase mĕqômî ûlĕʾarṣî (my place and my land) uses māqôm (H4725, place) — a term that acquired sacred resonance in Jacob's journey: the "place" where he slept at Bethel (28:11,16-17) and the "place" of Peniel (32:31). Jacob's homeland is both geographical and covenantally charged.
26: tĕnāh ʾet-nāšay wĕʾet-yĕlāḏay ʾăšer ʿāḇaḏtî ʾōtĕḵā bāhen wĕʾēlēḵāh — "Give me my wives and my children for whom I have served you, and I will go." The legal claim is precise: Jacob's wives and children are the result of his labor-contract (ʿāḇaḏtî ʾōtĕḵā bāhen, "I served you for them"). In the ANE labor-contract framework, a laborer who worked for a bride-price had rights to the bride and her children — they were legally his even if born in the employer's household. Jacob's claim is legally grounded: the fourteen years of labor have purchased his household, and he is entitled to take them.
27: niḥaštî wayyĕḇāreḵēnî YHWH biglālĕḵā — "I have divined that YHWH has blessed me because of you." Laban uses the verb nāḥaš (H5172, to practice divination, to observe signs, to divine) — a term for omen-reading and divination practices common in the ANE (Num 23:23; Deut 18:10 prohibit it for Israel). Laban is a diviner who has discerned through his oracular practices that Jacob's presence is the source of YHWH's blessing on his household. The irony: a Aramean diviner attributes his prosperity to the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. The theological truth he names correctly (YHWH has blessed me because of you) is the same truth Joseph's Egyptian masters will discern (39:3, 5).
28: nāqĕḇāh śĕḵārĕḵā ʿālay wĕʾettēnāh — "Name your wages and I will give it." Laban's invitation to Jacob to set his own wages is the same opening gambit as 29:15 — Laban invites Jacob to name terms in a negotiation Laban intends to control. The verb nāqaḇ (H5344, to pierce, to designate/specify) — "designate/specify your wages" — is a technical term for naming a price or figure explicitly. Laban will subsequently manipulate the terms (31:7: he changed Jacob's wages ten times), so the invitation to "name your wages" is less generous than it sounds.
29: ʾattāh yāḏaʿtā ʾēt ʾăšer ʿăḇaḏtîḵā wĕʾēt ʾăšer-hāyāh miqnĕḵā ʾittî — "You know how I have served you and how your livestock has fared with me." Jacob's appeal to Laban's own knowledge (ʾattāh yāḏaʿtā) is a rhetorical strategy — making Laban the acknowledging witness to Jacob's faithful labor. The verb yāḏaʿ (to know) here functions as evidence-appeal: you have seen, you know, the facts are as I state them. Jacob does not merely assert his good service; he calls Laban as his own witness. The livestock's prosperity "with me" (ʾittî) echoes the covenant formula "YHWH was with Jacob" — the implication is that YHWH's presence with Jacob produced the flourishing.
30: kî mĕʿaṭ ʾăšer-hāyāh lĕḵā lĕpānay wayyiprōṣ lārōḇ wayyĕḇāreḵ YHWH ʾōtĕḵā lĕraglî — "For the little you had before I came has grown greatly, and YHWH has blessed you wherever I walked." The phrase lĕraglî (literally "at my feet," meaning wherever I went/because of me) echoes the language of the covenant blessing through Abraham (12:3: "through you all nations will be blessed"). Jacob is the carrier of the Abrahamic blessing, and Laban has been the incidental beneficiary. Wayyiprōṣ lārōḇ — "he has burst forth greatly" — the verb pāraṣ (H6555, to break through, burst forth) used here will be applied to Jacob himself in 28:14 (your offspring will "burst forth" in all directions) and to Joseph's increase in Egypt.
31: lōʾ-tittēn-lî mĕʾûmāh ʾim-taʿăśeh-llî haddāḇār hazzeh — "You shall not give me anything; if you will do this one thing for me." Jacob's counter-proposal reverses Laban's offer: instead of a wage paid in existing livestock, Jacob will take only the future offspring of a specific category. The apparent generosity ("give me nothing") is the opening of a strategic arrangement. Mĕʾûmāh (H3972, anything, something) — the strongest negative: "not a single thing." Jacob's refusal of existing payment while claiming future increase reflects his confidence in divine provision (which will be confirmed in 31:10-13).
32: kol-śeh nāqōḏ wĕṭālûʾ wĕḵol-śeh-ḥûm baḵḵĕśāḇîm — "every speckled and spotted sheep and every dark lamb." The animal-classification system — speckled (nāqōḏ, H5348), spotted (ṭālûʾ, H2921), dark/black (ḥûm, H2345) — establishes the wage-category. The rarer colors (in a flock where solid-colored animals predominate) are Jacob's wages. Laban will remove all existing striped/spotted animals to his sons' care (v35), so Jacob begins with zero of the designated animals. His future increase will be entirely from the solid-colored breeding stock — which makes the subsequent proliferation of striped/spotted offspring miraculous.
33: wĕʿānĕtāh bî ṣiḏqātî bĕyôm māḥār — "and my honesty will answer for me in the future." Ṣiḏqāh (H6666, righteousness, justice, what is right) — Jacob appeals to his integrity (ṣĕḏāqāh) as the guarantor of the agreement. The phrase ʿānāh bî (answer for/testify against me) positions righteousness as a witness that will speak on Jacob's behalf — the same personification of righteousness as a covenant witness that appears in the Psalms and prophets (Ps 85:10-11: "righteousness and peace have kissed"). Jacob's appeal to his own righteousness before God is bold — it will be tested by the subsequent narrative.
34: hēn lû yĕhî kiḏĕḇāreḵā — "Good, let it be as you say." Laban's quick agreement — no negotiation, no counter-offer — reveals his confidence that the arrangement favors him. He has removed all existing striped animals to a three-day journey's distance (v35), ensuring Jacob starts with nothing. From Laban's perspective, the odds of sufficient striped offspring emerging from a solid-colored flock to constitute meaningful wages are low. Laban's confidence is entirely rational by ordinary agronomy; what he does not factor is divine intervention.
35: wayyāsar bayyôm hahûʾ ʾet-hattĕyāšîm hāʿăquddîm wĕhaṭṭĕlūʾîm — "That same day he removed all the male goats that were streaked or spotted." Laban's immediate action — removing all existing striped/spotted animals on the same day — closes every natural pathway to Jacob's wages. Hāʿăquddîm (striped/banded, H6124) and haṭṭĕlūʾîm (spotted, H2921) — the same categories Jacob proposed. Wayyittēn bĕyad-bānāyw — "he gave them into the hand of his sons" — Laban puts his own sons in charge of the separated flock, ensuring Jacob has no access. The three-day journey's distance (v36) creates a geographic buffer. Laban's manipulation is thorough; God's provision will be thorougher.
36: wayyāśem derek šĕlōšet yāmîm bênô ûḇên yaʿăqōḇ — "He put a three-day journey between himself and Jacob." Derek šĕlōšet yāmîm (three days' journey distance) is the maximum practical separation in pastoral travel — far enough that the flocks cannot intermingle or be secretly transferred. The same "three days' journey" will appear in the Exodus narrative (3:18; 5:3) for the distance into the wilderness Israel requests for worship — another connection between the Jacob-Laban narrative and the Exodus typology.
37: wayyiqqaḥ-lô yaʿăqōḇ maqqal liḇneh laḥ wĕlûz weʿarĕmôn wayyĕpaṣṣēl bāhen paṣṣĕlôt lĕḇānôt — "Jacob took fresh branches of poplar, almond, and plane trees and peeled white streaks on them." Jacob's branch-peeling technique — creating striped branches in the watering troughs — is folk-genetic practice based on the ancient belief that what a pregnant animal sees during conception affects the offspring's appearance. The three tree species (liḇneh = poplar/white wood; lûz = almond; ʿarĕmôn = plane/chestnut) produce white inner wood when stripped. Whether this technique could actually affect animal coloration is biologically questioned; the narrative's own answer comes in 31:10-12 where God reveals to Jacob in a dream that he (God) has been causing the striped offspring — the branches are not the mechanism.
38: wayyaṣṣēg ʾet-hammaqqĕlôt ʾăšer piṣṣēl bārehaṭîm bĕšiqĕtôt hammayim — "He placed the peeled branches in the troughs, in the watering channels, in front of the flocks." The watering troughs (rahaṭîm, H7298) are the site of Jacob's intervention. The connection to the well-meeting of ch 29 is deliberate: the same wells/troughs where Jacob met Rachel and rolled the stone are now the site of Jacob's prosperity scheme. The narrative geography is coherent. The timing — placing branches "when the flocks came to drink, since they mated when they came to drink" (baʿălōt haṣṣōʾn liḇôʾ lišĕtôt) — shows Jacob's understanding of pastoral seasonality.
39: wayyeḥĕmû haṣṣōʾn ʾel-hammaqqĕlôt wayyēlĕḏû haṣṣōʾn ʿăquddîm nĕquddîm ûṭĕluʾîm — "The flocks bred in front of the branches and bore streaked, speckled, and spotted young." The three result-categories match the three wage-categories Jacob proposed (v32): streaked (ʿăquddîm), speckled (nĕquddîm), spotted (ṭĕluʾîm). The supernatural proliferation of the minority-colored offspring defies ordinary agronomy — this is the point the narrative is building toward. Jacob's scheme (branches) appears to be the cause; God's dream-revelation (31:10-12) will clarify that the breeding was divinely orchestrated through Jacob's selective pairing, not through the branches' magical influence on conception.
40: wayyapred yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-haḵĕśāḇîm wayyittēn pĕnê haṣṣōʾn ʾel-ʿāqōḏ wĕḵol-ḥûm bĕṣōʾn lāḇān — "Jacob set apart the lambs and had the flocks face toward the streaked and dark animals of Laban." The selective pairing strategy — having the solid-colored females face striped/dark animals during mating — reflects Jacob's understanding of selective breeding, whatever folk-genetic theory underlies it. Wayĕhî-lô ʿăḏārîm lĕḇaddô — "he made separate flocks for himself and did not put them with Laban's" — Jacob maintains strict separation of his emerging flock from Laban's, preventing intermingling that would muddy the ownership claim.
41: wĕhāyāh bĕkol-yaḥēm haṣṣōʾn hamĕquššārôt wĕśām yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-hammaqqĕlôt lĕʿênê haṣṣōʾn bārehaṭîm lĕyaḥamnāh bammaqqĕlôt — "Whenever the stronger females were in heat, Jacob placed the branches in the troughs in front of the animals." The selective application — branches only for the "strong/vigorous" (meqûššārôt, H6989, knotted/vigorous) females — is Jacob's key economic insight: he reserves his breeding intervention for the productive stock. The Hebrew meqûššārôt is a hapax legomenon (appears only here) — possibly related to qāšar (to bind/be strong).
42: ûbĕhaʿăṭip haṣṣōʾn lōʾ yāśîm — "but for the weak animals he would not place them." The weak animals (haʿăṭupîm, H5848, the faint/feeble ones) produce Laban's share; the strong ones (hameqūšārôt) produce Jacob's. The result is that Jacob's flock is selectively composed of robust stock while Laban's declines in quality — an outcome that 31:9 attributes directly to God's action: "God has taken away your father's livestock and given them to me." The breeding scheme is presented as Jacob's stratagem, but the theological framing makes God the agent.
43: wayyiprōṣ hāʾîš mĕʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ wayhî-lô ṣōʾn rabbôt ûšĕpāḥôt waʿăḇāḏîm ûgĕmallîm waʿăḇāḏîm wăḥămōrîm — "So the man grew exceedingly great and came to have large flocks, and female and male servants, and camels and donkeys." The accumulation formula mĕʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ (very very much) — the same double superlative used for Abraham's covenant wealth (17:2) and Isaac's Philistine-envied prosperity (26:13-14). Jacob's prosperity follows the same covenant-blessing pattern. The four-category inventory (flocks, female servants, male servants, camels and donkeys) mirrors the wealth formulas used for Abraham (12:16; 24:35) — Jacob is being established as a third-generation carrier of the Abrahamic covenant prosperity. The chapter ends where it began, with the matriarchal rivalry — but now Jacob's prosperity is the narrative frame enclosing both the domestic strife and the divine provision.
